clinicalStem: A 52-year-old fair-skinned man presents with a pigmented lesion on his back excised and reported as melanoma with Breslow thickness of 2.8 mm, no ulceration, and no mitoses. Sentinel lymph node biopsy shows no nodal involvement and imaging reveals no distant metastases. He asks about his prognosis.
options: 5-year survival is generally less than 10%
5-year survival is greater than 50% to 90% given localized disease
5-year survival is 20% to 70% as this is stage III disease
Breslow thickness is no longer used; Clark level determines prognosis
Prognosis is the same regardless of Breslow thickness once nodes are negative
explanation: Localized melanoma with Breslow thickness greater than 1 mm carries a 5-year survival of greater than 50% to 90%. This patient has no nodal involvement and no distant metastasis, placing him in stage II (T3a N0 M0). Breslow thickness has replaced Clark level as the most important prognostic variable for primary tumor invasion. Distant metastatic disease carries less than 10% 5-year survival, and stage III (regional nodal disease) carries 20% to 70%.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Less than 10% 5-year survival applies to distant metastatic disease (stage IV), not localized disease.
Stage III survival of 20-70% requires nodal involvement, which this patient does not have.
Breslow thickness is currently the most important prognostic variable, having replaced Clark level.
Breslow thickness strongly predicts survival; nodal negativity is favorable but thickness still stratifies risk.
clinicalPearl: Breslow thickness is the single most important prognostic variable for primary cutaneous melanoma, predicting both metastatic risk and survival better than Clark level of invasion.
keyClue: No nodal involvement, no distant metastasis, Breslow 2.8 mm = localized disease with 50-90% 5-year survival.
commonExamTrap: Confusing stage III survival data (20-70%) with localized disease; nodal status must be confirmed before applying those figures.
missedConcept: Breslow thickness replaced Clark level as primary prognostic staging tool for melanoma.

subtopic: Excision margins and sentinel lymph node biopsy
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman has a melanoma on her forearm with Breslow thickness of 1.6 mm, no ulceration, and mitotic rate less than 1 per mm squared. Clinically lymph nodes are not palpable. What is the correct surgical management?
options: Wide local excision with 1 cm margin; no sentinel lymph node biopsy needed
Wide local excision with 2 cm margin and sentinel lymph node biopsy
Wide local excision with 5 mm margin; sentinel lymph node biopsy is not indicated for forearm tumors
Wide local excision with 2 to 3 cm margin and immediate completion lymph node dissection
Mohs micrographic surgery with 1 cm margin; no sentinel lymph node biopsy
explanation: For melanoma with Breslow thickness 1.0 to 4.0 mm, the recommended excision margin is 2 cm and sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is indicated. This patient's 1.6 mm thickness falls within this range. SLNB is not performed for tumors less than 1.0 mm unless high-risk features such as ulceration, lymphovascular invasion, age under 40, or high mitotic rate are present. Margins of 2-3 cm are used for tumors greater than 4.0 mm.
wrongAnswerExplanations: 1 cm margin is for tumors less than 1.0 mm; this patient's 1.6 mm tumor requires 2 cm margin plus SLNB.
5 mm margin is for in situ melanoma only; location on the forearm does not modify the standard margin.
Immediate completion lymph node dissection without SLNB first is not standard; SLNB is the initial nodal staging step.
Mohs surgery is used for complex facial or high-risk SCC/BCC, not standard for melanoma management.
clinicalPearl: Breslow 1.0-4.0 mm melanoma requires 2 cm excision margin and sentinel lymph node biopsy for nodal staging regardless of clinical lymph node status.
keyClue: Breslow 1.6 mm = 2 cm margin + SLNB. The absence of palpable nodes does not eliminate the need for SLNB.
commonExamTrap: Choosing 1 cm margin (reserved for <1 mm tumors) or omitting SLNB because nodes are clinically negative.
missedConcept: SLNB is offered for all melanomas 1.0-4.0 mm; clinically negative nodes do not change this recommendation.

clinicalStem: A 60-year-old dark-skinned man presents with a 3 cm flat hyperpigmented lesion with irregular borders and multiple color shades on the sole of his left foot. He has no history of significant sun exposure. Biopsy confirms melanoma.
options: Superficial spreading melanoma, the most common subtype arising from a preexisting nevus
Nodular melanoma arising de novo in normal skin with vertical growth pattern
Lentigo maligna, a carcinoma in situ related to chronic sun exposure on the head and neck
Acral lentiginous melanoma, most common in dark-skinned individuals, occurring on sun-protected sites
Amelanotic melanoma diagnosed only by immunohistochemical staining
explanation: Acral lentiginous melanoma occurs on palms, soles, subungual areas, and other sun-protected sites. It accounts for 35-60% of melanomas in dark-skinned individuals, making it the predominant subtype in this population, though rare in Caucasians. Lesions are typically 3 cm, flat with irregular borders and multiple color shades, and undergo a long radial growth phase before vertical growth increases metastatic risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Superficial spreading melanoma (50-70%) arises from preexisting nevi on sun-exposed skin; it is not predominant in dark-skinned individuals.
Nodular melanoma is dome-shaped (1-2 cm), arises de novo, is more common in men, and grows vertically from the start.
Lentigo maligna is a carcinoma in situ on head, face, and neck of women, clearly related to sun exposure; a sun-protected sole location excludes this.
Amelanotic melanoma has no visible pigment by light microscopy; this lesion is clearly hyperpigmented with multiple color shades.
clinicalPearl: Acral lentiginous melanoma is the most common melanoma subtype in dark-skinned individuals and arises on sun-protected sites (palms, soles, subungual). It is not sun-exposure related.
keyClue: Dark-skinned patient + sole of foot + no sun exposure history + flat irregular multicolored lesion = acral lentiginous melanoma.
commonExamTrap: Assuming superficial spreading melanoma because it is the most common overall; distribution and sun-exposure history must guide diagnosis.
missedConcept: Race and anatomical site are critical in subtyping melanoma; acral lentiginous melanoma predominates in dark-skinned populations.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old woman with previously excised melanoma presents with new lung nodules on CT imaging. Serum LDH is within normal limits. No other visceral metastases are found. What is her metastatic classification and expected prognosis?
options: M1a: metastases to skin/subcutaneous/distant nodes; 5-year survival greater than 50%
M1b: metastases to lung; 5-year survival generally less than 10%
M1c: any distant metastasis with elevated serum LDH; 5-year survival 20-70%
Stage III disease with regional nodal involvement; survival 20-70% depending on burden
Stage IIC with T4b primary; no distant spread confirmed
explanation: Isolated lung metastases in melanoma are classified as M1b. The 5-year survival for distant metastatic disease (stage IV) is generally less than 10%, regardless of the M subcategory. M1c applies to metastases to other visceral sites or any distant site combined with elevated serum LDH. M1a is skin, subcutaneous, or distant nodal metastases. This patient's normal LDH and lung-only disease confirms M1b classification.
wrongAnswerExplanations: M1a applies to skin, subcutaneous tissue, or distant lymph nodes, not pulmonary metastases.
M1c requires other visceral metastases or elevated LDH; this patient has normal LDH and lung-only disease.
Stage III indicates regional nodal involvement without distant metastases; lung metastases confirm stage IV.
Stage IIC (T4b N0 M0) is localized disease; the presence of lung metastases confirms distant spread and stage IV.
clinicalPearl: Melanoma M classification: M1a = skin/subcutaneous/distant nodes; M1b = lung; M1c = other viscera OR any distant site with elevated LDH. All stage IV carries less than 10% 5-year survival.
keyClue: Lung metastases only + normal LDH = M1b. Elevated LDH or other visceral sites would classify as M1c.
commonExamTrap: Classifying as M1c based on visceral metastasis without checking for elevated LDH; M1c requires visceral sites other than lung OR elevated LDH with any distant site.
missedConcept: Serum LDH level upgrades any distant metastasis to M1c regardless of site.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old man presents with a slowly enlarging indurated plaque on his right cheek that resembles a scar. There is no history of trauma or surgery to that area. The lesion has ill-defined borders and feels firm on palpation. No ulceration is visible.
options: Nodular BCC: most common subtype with pearly dome and telangiectasias
Superficial spreading BCC: found on the trunk, mimicking eczema or psoriasis
Morpheaform (sclerosing) BCC: most aggressive subtype, most difficult to diagnose clinically
Pigmented BCC: mimics melanoma with dark pigmentation
Marjolin ulcer: SCC arising from chronic inflammation or scarring
explanation: Morpheaform (sclerosing) BCC is the most aggressive BCC subtype. It presents as a scar-like indurated plaque on the head and neck, with ill-defined borders and no obvious ulceration, making it the most difficult subtype to diagnose clinically. The absence of trauma history in a scar-like facial lesion is a key clinical clue. Its ill-defined margins make complete excision challenging.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Nodular BCC presents as a pearly dome-shaped nodule with rolled borders and telangiectasias, not a scar-like plaque.
Superficial spreading BCC occurs on the trunk and extremities as an erythematous patch mimicking fungal infection, eczema, or psoriasis.
Pigmented BCC appears with dark pigmentation mimicking melanoma or seborrheic keratosis, not as a scar-like plaque.
Marjolin ulcer is SCC arising in chronic wounds or burn scars and typically presents as an ulcerative lesion in a site of chronic inflammation.
clinicalPearl: Morpheaform BCC presents as a scar-like plaque with no trauma history on the face or head and neck. It is the most aggressive BCC subtype and the most difficult to diagnose clinically due to ill-defined borders.
keyClue: Scar-like plaque + no trauma history + face/head and neck = morpheaform BCC until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Mistaking this for nodular BCC or a post-surgical scar; the absence of any prior trauma or surgery is the key distinguishing clue.
missedConcept: Morpheaform BCC lacks the classic BCC features of pearly nodule and telangiectasias, making clinical recognition difficult.

subtopic: SCC risk factors and malignant transformation
clinicalStem: A 50-year-old male renal transplant recipient presents with a rapidly enlarging keratotic plaque on his forearm with recent ulceration and bleeding. He was transplanted 8 years ago and is maintained on tacrolimus and mycophenolate. Biopsy reveals well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma.
options: Immunosuppression after organ transplantation increases SCC risk by approximately 10 times
Post-transplant immunosuppression increases cutaneous SCC risk by approximately 250 times compared to the general population
Cyclosporine causes direct UV damage and is the sole cause of post-transplant SCC
SCC in transplant recipients has the same prognosis as SCC in immunocompetent patients
Post-transplant SCC arises exclusively from Bowen disease and not from actinic keratosis
explanation: Post-transplant immunosuppression increases the risk of cutaneous SCC by approximately 250 times compared to the general population. Chronic immunosuppression impairs immune surveillance of malignant cells and allows oncogenic viruses such as HPV to drive tumor formation. SCC in transplant recipients tends to be more aggressive, with higher rates of local recurrence and metastasis compared to immunocompetent patients.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 10-fold increase grossly underestimates the risk; the actual risk is approximately 250 times higher.
Cyclosporine has direct oncogenic effects but immunosuppression as a class (not one drug) is responsible; UV is an additional cofactor, not the mechanism attributed solely to cyclosporine.
Post-transplant SCC carries a worse prognosis with higher metastatic rates than in immunocompetent individuals.
SCC in transplant recipients arises from actinic keratoses and other precursors, not exclusively from Bowen disease.
clinicalPearl: Organ transplant recipients have approximately 250 times the risk of cutaneous SCC due to long-term immunosuppression, and their SCCs tend to be more aggressive with higher metastatic potential.
keyClue: Transplant recipient + chronic immunosuppression + keratotic ulcerating lesion on sun-exposed skin = high-risk SCC with 250x risk elevation.
commonExamTrap: Underestimating the magnitude of risk increase; 250-fold is the lecture-specified figure, not 10- or 20-fold.
missedConcept: Post-transplant SCC is more aggressive than in immunocompetent patients and requires vigilant surveillance and aggressive management.

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old woman is diagnosed with nodular basal cell carcinoma on her nose measuring 0.8 cm. She is concerned about the cancer spreading to her lymph nodes and asks about the risk of metastasis and whether additional workup is needed.
options: BCC metastasizes in approximately 5-10% of cases; staging CT of neck and chest is required
BCC rarely metastasizes (less than 0.05%); no additional workup is needed after excision
BCC has a 15% metastatic rate comparable to SCC; sentinel lymph node biopsy is recommended
The metastatic rate for BCC depends on Breslow thickness; staging applies the melanoma protocol
BCC of the face metastasizes in 20% of cases; orbital exenteration may be needed
explanation: Basal cell carcinoma is locally aggressive but rarely metastasizes; the metastatic rate is less than 0.05%. Because metastasis is so rare, no additional staging workup is needed after complete excision. For tumors less than 1 cm, the recommended surgical margin is 5 mm. BCC does not use Breslow thickness or sentinel lymph node biopsy protocols, which are specific to melanoma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A 5-10% metastatic rate and staging CT are not applicable to BCC; this significantly overestimates BCC metastatic potential.
A 15% metastatic rate and SLNB apply to certain SCC risk groups, not BCC.
BCC staging uses its own clinical assessment; Breslow thickness and melanoma staging protocols do not apply.
A 20% facial metastatic rate and orbital exenteration are not supported; BCC metastasis is extremely rare at less than 0.05%.
clinicalPearl: BCC is locally destructive but almost never metastasizes (less than 0.05%). No additional staging workup is required after excision. For tumors less than 1 cm, a 5 mm excision margin is adequate.
keyClue: BCC = very low metastatic risk (less than 0.05%). Reassure and excise; no staging CT or SLNB needed.
commonExamTrap: Applying melanoma or SCC staging protocols to BCC; BCC requires no additional staging workup due to its negligible metastatic rate.
missedConcept: BCC mortality is rare despite local aggression; its main risk is local recurrence and destruction, not systemic metastasis.

clinicalStem: A 25-year-old woman presents with a slowly enlarging subcutaneous nodule near her right lateral eyebrow present since childhood. It is smooth, non-tender, and does not transilluminate. The surgeon plans excision. Which of the following is a recognized complication of incomplete excision and a reason why complete removal is mandatory?
options: Malignant transformation is not a risk; incomplete excision only causes local recurrence without further consequence
Dermoid cysts can undergo malignant change, making complete removal mandatory to prevent this complication
Dermoid cysts are lined by stratified squamous epithelium only and cannot transform; excision is cosmetic
The main risk of incomplete excision is rupture causing chemical peritonitis due to the cyst contents
Dermoid cysts in the lateral eyebrow region regress spontaneously and do not require excision
explanation: Dermoid cysts are congenital lesions found predominantly on the head and neck. They can undergo malignant change, which makes complete removal mandatory. When excising dermoid cysts near the temporal region, care must be taken not to injure the temporal branch of the facial nerve. Incomplete excision carries the risk of recurrence and malignant transformation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Malignant transformation is a recognized risk of dermoid cysts and is the primary reason why complete excision is mandatory.
Dermoid cysts do contain stratified squamous epithelium but also adnexal structures; the risk of malignant transformation is not negated by their lining.
Chemical peritonitis is a risk of dermoid cysts in the ovary, not cutaneous dermoid cysts of the head and neck.
Dermoid cysts do not regress spontaneously; they slowly enlarge and require complete surgical excision.
clinicalPearl: Dermoid cysts can undergo malignant transformation, mandating complete removal. When operating near the lateral eyebrow/temporal region, protect the temporal branch of the facial nerve.
keyClue: Congenital subcutaneous nodule near eyebrow since childhood + malignant transformation risk = dermoid cyst requiring complete excision.
commonExamTrap: Confusing dermoid cyst with epidermoid (sebaceous) cyst; epidermoid cysts carry low malignancy risk, while dermoid cysts have recognized malignant change potential.
missedConcept: Dermoid cysts differ from epidermoid cysts in origin, location, and malignant potential; temporal branch of facial nerve is at risk during excision.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old man undergoes excision of a lipoma on his upper back. Three months later he returns with a recurrence in the same location. The surgeon suspects incomplete removal. What is the most important technical principle that should have been followed to prevent local recurrence?
options: The lipoma should have been removed using liposuction to ensure complete cavity clearance
The lipoma must be excised together with its capsule to prevent local recurrence
Lipomas always recur regardless of surgical technique; re-excision is futile
A 1 cm margin of surrounding normal fat should have been excised with the lipoma
CO2 laser ablation should have been used instead of surgical excision to prevent recurrence
explanation: Lipoma is the most common mesenchymal soft tissue tumor. Complete excision with the capsule intact is essential to prevent local recurrence. Leaving the capsule behind allows residual tumor cells to proliferate and causes recurrence. CO2 laser or liposuction does not reliably remove the capsule and is not the standard approach. Wide margins of normal tissue are not required for benign lipoma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Liposuction does not remove the capsule and has a higher recurrence rate than open excision with capsule removal.
Lipomas can be cured by complete excision with their capsule; recurrence is not inevitable.
Wide margins of normal fat are not required; the key is capsule excision, not margins of normal tissue.
CO2 laser is used for neurofibroma in NF1/NF2, not lipomas; it does not address the capsule.
clinicalPearl: Lipoma recurrence after surgery is caused by leaving the capsule behind. Complete en bloc excision including the capsule is the definitive treatment.
keyClue: Lipoma recurrence = incomplete capsule removal. Must excise lipoma with its capsule to cure.
commonExamTrap: Assuming recurrence is inevitable or that liposuction is equivalent; the capsule must be removed in its entirety.
missedConcept: Lipoma is the most common mesenchymal tumor; complete capsule removal is the only way to prevent recurrence.

clinicalStem: A newborn infant is noted to have a dark brown hairy nevus measuring 22 cm across the lower back. The parents ask about management options and the risk of malignant change.
options: This is a small congenital nevus (less than 1.5 cm); observation alone is appropriate with annual review
This is a giant congenital pigmented nevus (greater than 20 cm); management requires serial excision, skin grafting, or flap reconstruction due to significant malignant potential
Congenital nevi never undergo malignant change; reassure and discharge
This is a medium congenital nevus (1.5-20 cm); simple excision at 6 months of age is curative
Laser ablation with CO2 laser is the first-line treatment for giant congenital nevi and is curative
explanation: Congenital pigmented nevi are classified by size: small (less than 1.5 cm), medium (1.5-20 cm), and giant (greater than 20 cm). A 22 cm nevus is a giant congenital pigmented nevus, which carries significant risk of malignant transformation (into melanoma). Management requires serial excision with primary closure, skin grafting, or flap reconstruction depending on size and location. Observation alone or simple excision is insufficient given the malignant risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: At 22 cm, this is a giant nevus (greater than 20 cm), not small (less than 1.5 cm).
Congenital nevi, particularly giant ones, carry significant malignant potential; observation alone is inappropriate.
At 22 cm this is a giant nevus (greater than 20 cm), not medium (1.5-20 cm); simple excision would be inadequate.
CO2 laser does not achieve complete nevus removal and does not address melanoma risk; it is not standard for giant congenital nevi.
clinicalPearl: Giant congenital pigmented nevi (greater than 20 cm) carry significant melanoma risk and require serial excision with skin grafting or flap reconstruction. Size classification: small less than 1.5 cm, medium 1.5-20 cm, giant greater than 20 cm.
keyClue: 22 cm hairy nevus at birth = giant congenital nevus (greater than 20 cm) with significant malignant potential requiring complex reconstruction.
commonExamTrap: Misclassifying size or recommending observation; giant congenital nevi have substantial melanoma risk and require active surgical management.
missedConcept: Size classification of congenital nevi is critical: giant (greater than 20 cm) warrants serial excision/reconstruction due to malignant potential.

subtopic: Timing of palate repair and speech outcomes
clinicalStem: A 3-year-old child with isolated cleft palate, repaired at 18 months of age, is referred by a speech therapist for persistent nasal air escape and compensatory articulation errors. The parents report speech was never normal after repair. Which best explains the primary speech complication in this child?
options: Delayed palate repair beyond 12 months causes maladaptive speech errors due to inadequate velopharyngeal function during early speech development
Palate repair at 18 months is too early and should have been deferred to age 5 for better functional outcomes
Speech problems after cleft palate repair are always caused by hearing loss from otitis media
Isolated cleft palate never causes speech problems as long as the hard palate is repaired
Nasal air escape after palate repair indicates a surgical failure that cannot be treated further
explanation: An intact velopharyngeal sphincter is necessary to develop normal speech mechanics. When palate repair is delayed beyond early speech development (typically 12 months), a child will develop maladaptive compensatory errors that are difficult to correct later. This child's repair at 18 months exceeded the critical window, contributing to persistent velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) and compensatory articulation. The lecture states cleft palate repair should occur before 12 months when critical language develops.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Palate repair at 18 months is too late (beyond the 12-month critical window), not too early; later repair worsens speech outcomes.
Hearing loss from otitis media is a complication of cleft palate due to Eustachian tube dysfunction but is not the primary explanation for compensatory articulation errors.
Soft palate clefting disrupts the velar sling and causes oronasal airflow problems; speech is always affected without repair.
Persistent VPI after repair can be treated with additional speech surgery such as pharyngoplasty; it is not untreatable.
clinicalPearl: Cleft palate repair should occur before 12 months of age to prevent maladaptive speech errors. Delay beyond this critical window leads to compensatory articulation that is difficult to remediate even after late repair.
keyClue: Palate repair at 18 months (after 12-month critical window) + compensatory speech errors + VPI = consequence of delayed repair.
commonExamTrap: Thinking early repair is always harmful to facial growth; the speech cost of delay beyond 12 months outweighs the growth concern in most cases.
missedConcept: The 12-month critical window for palate repair balances speech development against maxillofacial growth; repair after this window leads to worse speech outcomes.

topic: Cleft lip and palate epidemiology and recurrence risk
clinicalStem: A couple attends genetic counseling following the birth of their first child with non-syndromic cleft lip and palate. Both parents are unaffected. They are planning a second pregnancy and ask about the risk to future children. No syndrome has been identified.
options: The recurrence risk is 1%, as cleft lip and palate is almost exclusively a de novo mutation with negligible familial risk
If one child has CL/P with unaffected parents, the recurrence risk for subsequent children is approximately 4%
The risk is 25%, consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance
The risk is 50%, consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance with complete penetrance
The risk is 17%, which applies when two children are already affected
explanation: For non-syndromic CL/P, when one child is affected and both parents are unaffected, the recurrence risk for subsequent siblings is approximately 4%. The risk increases to 9% if two previous children are affected, and to 17% if one parent and one child are both affected. CL/P is multifactorial, not following simple Mendelian patterns, so risks are empirically derived rather than calculated as 25% or 50%.
wrongAnswerExplanations: 1% underestimates the risk; the empirically derived recurrence risk after one affected child is 4%.
25% would imply autosomal recessive inheritance; CL/P is multifactorial, not Mendelian recessive.
50% would imply autosomal dominant inheritance; CL/P does not follow this pattern.
17% applies when one parent AND one child are both affected, not when parents are unaffected with one affected child.
clinicalPearl: Non-syndromic CL/P recurrence risk: 4% if one child affected (parents unaffected); 9% if two children affected; 17% if one parent and one child affected. The risk increases with more affected family members.
keyClue: One affected child + both parents unaffected + non-syndromic = 4% recurrence risk for next pregnancy.
commonExamTrap: Applying Mendelian ratios (25% or 50%) to a multifactorial condition; CL/P risks are empirically derived and depend on family loading.
missedConcept: CL/P recurrence risk is multifactorial and empirically derived; increasing number of affected relatives progressively raises the risk.
